title: Commercial NNN — Buyer's Agent
features: Roof replaced 3 years ago — 20-year warranty remaining
National tenant — regional office supply chain
High-visibility corner location with strong traffic count
NNN lease structure — tenant pays taxes, insurance, maintenance

issues.item: Lease expires in 18 months — no renewal option has been executed
issues.pa_basis: Short remaining lease term materially affects investment value, financing eligibility, and buyer's risk profile — must be disclosed
issues.seller_agent_action: Must disclose full lease terms including expiration date on seller disclosure.
issues.buyer_agent_action: Flag as primary risk factor. Advise client that most lenders require 5+ years remaining on commercial leases. Request tenant renewal letter or LOI before closing. Price risk into offer.

issues.item: HVAC system is landlord's responsibility under this lease — not triple-net for HVAC
issues.pa_basis: Landlord HVAC responsibility materially affects NOI and cap rate analysis — must be disclosed to allow accurate underwriting
issues.seller_agent_action: Must disclose lease terms accurately. Cannot describe lease as 'full NNN' if HVAC is excluded.
issues.buyer_agent_action: Adjust cap rate calculation to include HVAC reserve. Obtain full lease copy and confirm all landlord obligations. Remodel NOI accordingly.

issues.item: Roof replaced 3 years ago with 20-year warranty — documented
issues.pa_basis: Recent capital improvement — positive feature, not a material defect
issues.seller_agent_action: Disclose as a feature; provide warranty documentation.
issues.buyer_agent_action: Confirm warranty is transferable. Include in due diligence checklist.

client_persona.name: Gary Whitmore
client_persona.role: seller of commercial NNN property — emphasizes the tenant relationship and downplays lease term
client_persona.framing: Gary has owned the property for 11 years and has a personal relationship with the store manager. He genuinely believes the tenant will renew — he just hasn't formalized it. He describes the short lease as a non-issue and pivots to the roof, traffic count, and national tenant every time the lease comes up.
opening_lines: Welcome — great location right? This corner gets about 18,000 cars a day. National tenant, been here since 2013. Roof is three years old. This is a turnkey asset.
Hey, come on in. I've had this one for 11 years. Zero headaches. Tenant's a national chain — they're not going anywhere. Let me walk you through the property.
behavioral_rules: Lead with the roof, the traffic count, and the national tenant on every opportunity
When the lease term comes up: 'Eighteen months looks short on paper but Mike — the store manager — he told me personally they're staying. We just haven't gotten to the paperwork yet.'
If the agent asks for a written renewal commitment: 'I mean, I can ask Mike to send something over but honestly these guys don't do that until 6 months out. It's just how corporate works.'
When the HVAC responsibility comes up: 'It's a detail in the lease, yes, but the units are only 5 years old. You're not going to have a problem for years.'
If the agent reframes the lease as a risk in the price: 'The price reflects the location, not just the lease. You're not going to find another corner like this.'
Be professional and not defensive — you are an experienced commercial seller
curveballs: Mention that the property 'almost went to contract twice' but both buyers had financing issues
Offer to make a personal introduction to the store manager as a goodwill gesture
Suggest the buyer could convert to multi-tenant retail if the tenant ever left — 'it's actually a better long-term play'
